questions.question: What is the main job of a Payload Converter in Temporal?

choices.text: To encrypt workflow history so that no one can read it at rest
To serialize your application objects into a binary Payload and deserialize them back
To route tasks to the correct Worker based on the data type being processed
To compress large workflow inputs so they fit within Temporal's size limits

questions.explanation: A Payload Converter converts your application's data (like Python objects or Go structs) into a binary form called a Payload — which is what Temporal actually transports between the client, server, and workers. The reverse conversion happens when the data is received on the other end. This is separate from encryption (handled by Payload Codecs) and has nothing to do with routing or compression.

questions.question: When a Composite Data Converter has multiple Payload Converters configured, how does it decide which one to use for a given value?

choices.text: It picks the converter whose name most closely matches the data type being serialized
It runs all converters in parallel and uses the result from the fastest one
It randomly selects a converter and falls back to the next one if an error occurs
It tries each converter in order and uses the first one that successfully handles the value

questions.explanation: The Composite Data Converter passes the value through each Payload Converter in sequence. The first converter that returns a non-nil (or successful) Payload 'wins' and is used. This is why ordering matters — if a more general converter like JSON comes before a more specific one, it might handle values that your custom converter was meant to handle first.

questions.question: Why does the documentation recommend that Workflow inputs use a single dataclass or Pydantic model instead of multiple separate parameters?

choices.text: Temporal's SDK only supports a single input parameter and will throw an error with multiple arguments
A single model lets you add optional fields with defaults later without breaking existing callers
Using a single model improves serialization speed because fewer conversions are needed
Multiple parameters are not supported for Signals and Queries, only for Workflows

questions.explanation: While the SDK technically allows multiple parameters, a single dataclass or model is strongly preferred for forward compatibility. When you need to add a new field to a workflow's interface later, you can add it with a default value on the model without changing the method signature — existing callers that don't pass the new field will still work. This is a common practice for evolving APIs safely over time.

questions.question: What does a Payload Codec do that is different from a Payload Converter?

choices.text: A Payload Codec transforms already-serialized Payloads (for example, to encrypt or compress them), while a Payload Converter handles the initial object-to-binary serialization
A Payload Codec converts application objects to JSON, while a Payload Converter handles binary protobuf encoding
A Payload Codec is used only on the Worker side, while a Payload Converter is used only on the client side
A Payload Codec defines the schema for workflow inputs, while a Payload Converter enforces that schema at runtime

questions.explanation: The two components operate at different layers. A Payload Converter turns your application objects into Payloads (the serialization step). A Payload Codec then transforms those already-serialized Payloads — for example, to encrypt or compress them before they leave the process. Importantly, the documentation notes these two can be customized independently of each other, giving you flexibility over each layer separately.

questions.question: When would you need to create a custom Payload Converter instead of relying on the default one?

choices.text: Whenever your workflow runs for more than a few seconds, since long-running workflows require custom serialization
Only when using the Go SDK, because the Python SDK's default converter handles all types automatically
When your application uses data types that the default converter does not support natively
Whenever you use Signals or Queries, because those always require explicit serialization configuration

questions.explanation: The default Data Converter handles many common types out of the box — None, bytes, protobuf messages, and JSON-serializable types in Python, for example. You only need a custom converter when your data includes types outside that supported set, like a custom network address type (IPv4Address) or some other domain-specific object that the default JSON serializer wouldn't know how to handle correctly.

questions.question: If you want to add support for a custom type but still keep all the default conversion behavior, what is the recommended approach?

choices.text: Replace the entire default Data Converter with a new one that only contains your custom converter
Insert your custom converter into the ordered list alongside the default converters, typically before the JSON fallback
Subclass the default JSON converter and override its entire serialization logic from scratch
Configure a separate Data Converter only for the specific workflow that uses the custom type

questions.explanation: The recommended pattern is to build a new Composite Data Converter that includes both your custom converter and all the original default converters. By placing your custom converter just before the general JSON converter (which acts as a fallback for everything else), you extend the default behavior rather than replacing it. This way, all the types that worked before still work, and your new type is also supported.

questions.question: In Go, you want to add a custom Payload Converter but still use the default converters as fallbacks. Which pattern correctly inserts your custom converter just before the default JSON fallthrough?

choices.text: converter.NewCompositeDataConverter(YourCustomPayloadConverter()) — passing only your converter causes Temporal to append the default converters automatically after it

converter.NewCompositeDataConverter(converter.NewNilPayloadConverter(), converter.NewByteSlicePayloadConverter(), converter.NewProtoJSONPayloadConverter(), converter.NewProtoPayloadConverter(), YourCustomPayloadConverter(), converter.NewJSONPayloadConverter()) — explicitly list all defaults with your converter placed before NewJSONPayloadConverter
converter.NewCompositeDataConverter(converter.NewJSONPayloadConverter(), YourCustomPayloadConverter()) — JSON must come first so your converter can override specific types while JSON handles the rest

converter.NewCompositeDataConverter(YourCustomPayloadConverter(), converter.NewNilPayloadConverter(), converter.NewByteSlicePayloadConverter(), converter.NewProtoJSONPayloadConverter(), converter.NewProtoPayloadConverter(), converter.NewJSONPayloadConverter()) — placing your converter first ensures it is tried before any built-in converter

questions.explanation: The Go SDK's CompositeDataConverter applies converters in the exact order they are listed, stopping at the first one that returns a non-nil Payload. To keep the default behavior intact while adding a custom converter, you must explicitly list all the default converters and insert yours just before NewJSONPayloadConverter — the JSON converter is intentionally the 'catch-all' fallthrough. Option A is wrong because passing only your converter discards all defaults; the SDK does not append them automatically. Option C reverses the order so JSON would swallow everything before your converter ever runs. Option D puts your converter first, meaning it would be tried before Nil, ByteSlice, and Proto converters, which could cause unexpected behavior for those types.

questions.question: In the Go SDK's default Data Converter, in what order are the built-in Payload Converters applied?

choices.text: JSON → ProtoJSON → Proto → ByteSlice → Nil, so that the most common type (JSON) is tried first to minimize conversion overhead
Nil → JSON → ByteSlice → ProtoJSON → Proto, prioritizing nil and generic JSON before binary formats
ProtoJSON → Proto → Nil → ByteSlice → JSON, because protobuf types are more common in production workflows
Nil → ByteSlice → ProtoJSON → Proto → JSON, with JSON last so it acts as the catch-all fallthrough for any otherwise unhandled type

questions.explanation: The Go SDK default Data Converter applies converters in this exact sequence: NilPayloadConverter, ByteSlicePayloadConverter, ProtoJSONPayloadConverter, ProtoPayloadConverter, and finally JSONPayloadConverter. JSON is intentionally last because it can handle almost any serializable type — placing it last makes it the catch-all so more specific converters (Nil, byte slices, Protobuf) get first priority. Option A reverses the order. Option B puts JSON second, skipping ByteSlice and Proto first. Option C starts with Proto types, which is not the defined default order.

questions.question: In Python, you need to use `datetime.date` and `datetime.datetime` objects as Workflow parameters. Which approach correctly enables this?

choices.text: Use the default DataConverter — datetime types are JSON-serializable via `json.dumps()` and are natively handled by the DefaultPayloadConverter without any extra configuration
Write a custom EncodingPayloadConverter that serializes datetime objects to ISO strings, register it before the default JSON converter in a CompositePayloadConverter
Use the Pydantic Data Converter by installing Pydantic and passing `pydantic_data_converter` as the `data_converter` argument when creating the Client
Wrap datetime values in a dataclass and use the default DataConverter, since dataclasses are supported and their fields are resolved through the JSON converter

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states that `datetime.date`, `datetime.time`, and `datetime.datetime` can *only* be used with the Pydantic Data Converter. They are not handled by the DefaultPayloadConverter. Option A is wrong — although some types are JSON-serializable, datetime objects are not among the types natively supported by the default converter. Option B would work technically but is unnecessary extra effort when the Pydantic Data Converter already supports the full datetime module. Option D is also incorrect — wrapping a datetime in a dataclass doesn't change how the inner datetime field is serialized; the default JSON converter still can't handle it.

questions.question: A Python developer implements a custom `EncodingPayloadConverter` for `IPv4Address` types but notices the type doesn't work when used inside a `List[IPv4Address]` parameter. What is the recommended fix?

choices.text: Register the `IPv4AddressEncodingPayloadConverter` with a higher priority index in the CompositePayloadConverter so it intercepts list elements before the JSON converter processes the outer list

Customize the JSON converter by providing a custom `JSONEncoder` and `JSONTypeConverter`, then replace the default `JSONPlainPayloadConverter` with the customized version in the CompositePayloadConverter
Switch to the Pydantic Data Converter, which natively handles `IPv4Address` inside collections because Pydantic resolves all standard library types
Convert the `List[IPv4Address]` to `List[str]` at the Workflow boundary and reconstruct the IPv4Address objects inside the Workflow function after deserialization

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly calls out this exact scenario: when you need a custom type to work inside lists, unions, and other collections, you should customize the existing JSON converter rather than adding a separate encoding converter. The JSON converter handles complex nested types, so you need to extend it with a custom `JSONEncoder` for serialization and a `JSONTypeConverter` for deserialization. Option A won't help because the issue is that the JSON converter sees a list and recursively serializes elements using its own logic — a separate encoding converter never gets a chance to handle inner elements. Option C is incorrect because `IPv4Address` is not a Pydantic or standard library type that the Pydantic Data Converter covers by default. Option D works around the problem but is an ugly hack that pollutes Workflow interfaces.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes the relationship between a Payload Converter and a Payload Codec in Temporal's data conversion architecture?

choices.text: A Payload Codec replaces the Payload Converter — once you configure a codec (e.g., for encryption), Temporal bypasses the converter layer entirely and the codec handles both serialization and encoding
Payload Converters and Payload Codecs operate at the same layer and are configured together as a single unit; you cannot customize one without also replacing the other

Payload Converters and Payload Codecs can be customized independently of each other — converters handle type serialization while codecs handle encoding/compression/encryption of the resulting Payload bytes
A Payload Codec is only relevant for Protobuf types; for JSON workflows the Payload Converter alone handles both serialization and any required encryption or compression

questions.explanation: Both the Go and Python documentation state explicitly that 'Payload Converters can be customized independently of a Payload Codec.' Converters are responsible for transforming application types into the Payload format (serialization), while Codecs operate on the resulting Payload bytes for purposes like compression or encryption. This separation lets you, for example, add a custom type converter without touching your encryption codec, or add an encryption codec without changing your serialization logic. Option A is wrong — codecs don't replace converters; they operate at a different layer. Option B is wrong because they are independently customizable. Option D is wrong because Codecs apply to all Payload types, not just Protobuf.

questions.question: In Python, a team defines a Workflow signal with three separate parameters: `(user_id: str, action: str, metadata: dict)`. What does the documentation recommend instead, and why?

choices.text: Keep three parameters — Python's type hints allow the default converter to handle each individually, and multiple parameters improve readability in handler signatures
Combine them into a single `dataclass` or Pydantic model parameter, so fields with defaults can be added later without breaking backward compatibility with callers using the old signature
Combine them into a `dict` parameter — dicts are JSON-serializable by default and allow schema evolution without changing the function signature
Use `*args` and `**kwargs` in the signal definition so that the number and names of parameters can evolve freely as requirements change over time

questions.explanation: The Python documentation explicitly recommends using a single `dataclass` or Pydantic model parameter for Workflows, Updates, Signals, and Queries. The reason is schema evolution: if you add a new optional field with a default value to a dataclass, existing callers that don't send that field still work correctly. With multiple positional parameters, adding a new parameter is a breaking change. Option A is wrong because it ignores the compatibility concern. Option C works at the serialization level but a raw `dict` loses type safety and doesn't benefit from Temporal's type-hint-based deserialization. Option D using `*args/**kwargs` is not a recommended Temporal pattern and Temporal's SDK can't apply type hints for deserialization to variadic arguments.

questions.question: When implementing a custom `EncodingPayloadConverter` in Python, what should `to_payload()` return when the converter does NOT handle the given value?

choices.text: Raise a `TypeError` so the CompositePayloadConverter knows to stop trying further converters and surface the error to the caller immediately
Return an empty `Payload()` object with no data, signaling that the value is intentionally left as an empty message
Call `super().to_payload(value)` to delegate to the base EncodingPayloadConverter's default handling logic
Return `None` so the CompositePayloadConverter knows to pass the value to the next converter in the sequence

questions.explanation: Looking at the Python example in the documentation, the `to_payload` method returns `None` when the value is not an instance of the handled type (e.g., `IPv4Address`). This signals to the `CompositePayloadConverter` that this converter doesn't handle the value, and the composite will move on to the next converter in the sequence. This is the same pattern used in Go. Option A is wrong — raising an exception would terminate conversion entirely rather than allowing fallthrough. Option B is wrong — an empty Payload is a valid result that would be accepted as the serialized form of the value, not a 'skip' signal. Option C is wrong — the base `EncodingPayloadConverter` doesn't implement `to_payload` in a way that handles arbitrary types.

questions.question: A team wants to use Pydantic models in their Python Temporal workflows. After running `pip install pydantic`, they create a Client but forget to configure the data converter. What happens when a Pydantic model is passed as a Workflow argument?

choices.text: It works without issue — Pydantic models are dataclasses under the hood and the default JSON converter handles them transparently via `json.dumps()`
Serialization succeeds because Pydantic models are JSON-serializable, but deserialization likely fails or produces a plain `dict` instead of the Pydantic model instance on the other side
Temporal raises a configuration error at startup because it detects Pydantic is installed but `pydantic_data_converter` is not set
The default converter silently falls back to pickle serialization for Pydantic models, which works but may cause issues across different Python versions

questions.explanation: Pydantic models can serialize to JSON (Pydantic provides `.model_dump()` and `.dict()`), but the default Temporal `DefaultPayloadConverter` uses standard `json.dumps()` logic combined with dataclass handling — it is not aware of Pydantic's specific type system. On deserialization, Temporal won't know to reconstruct a Pydantic model from the raw JSON dict, so you'd likely get a plain `dict` back rather than a typed model instance. The documentation makes it explicit that you must set `pydantic_data_converter` on the Client to properly use Pydantic models. Option A is wrong because Pydantic models are not standard Python dataclasses. Option C is wrong — Temporal does not inspect installed packages at startup. Option D is wrong — the default converter never uses pickle.

questions.question: Your Go Temporal workers handle a mix of Protobuf messages and custom domain objects. After deploying a new Composite Data Converter, you notice that Protobuf payloads are being serialized as generic JSON blobs instead of compact binary proto. Inspecting your converter setup, you find it is configured as: `NewCompositeDataConverter(NewProtoJSONPayloadConverter(), YourCustomPayloadConverter(), NewProtoPayloadConverter(), NewJSONPayloadConverter())`. What is the most likely cause?

choices.text: `NewProtoJSONPayloadConverter()` comes before `NewProtoPayloadConverter()` in the chain, so Protobuf messages match the JSON proto converter first and are never passed to the binary proto converter.
The custom `YourCustomPayloadConverter()` intercepts all non-nil values before the Protobuf binary converter can run, causing Protobuf messages to fall through to JSON.
`NewCompositeDataConverter` ignores Protobuf-specific converters when a custom converter is present, always falling back to JSON for structured types.
The absence of `NewNilPayloadConverter()` as the first entry causes the serialization chain to short-circuit before reaching the binary proto converter.

questions.explanation: The Composite Data Converter tries each Payload Converter in order and stops at the first one that returns a non-nil Payload. `NewProtoJSONPayloadConverter()` handles Protobuf messages and returns a non-nil Payload (as JSON), so `NewProtoPayloadConverter()` (binary) is never reached. To get compact binary proto, `NewProtoPayloadConverter()` must appear before `NewProtoJSONPayloadConverter()` in the chain. This ordering sensitivity is a frequent source of serialization surprises in production.

questions.question: A Python Temporal workflow uses `datetime.date` fields in its input dataclass. Workers running the default `DefaultPayloadConverter` consistently fail to deserialize these fields on receipt, raising a type conversion error. The workflow worked fine with plain `str` fields. What change resolves this without altering the workflow's data model?

choices.text: Register a custom `EncodingPayloadConverter` that encodes `datetime.date` as ISO-8601 strings and add it ahead of the default converters in a `CompositePayloadConverter`.
Switch to using `datetime.datetime` instead of `datetime.date`, since the default converter only supports the full datetime type from the standard library.
Configure the client with `pydantic_data_converter`, since `datetime.date`, `datetime.time`, and `datetime.datetime` are only supported by the Pydantic Data Converter.
Annotate the dataclass field with `temporalio.common.RawValue` so the default JSON converter treats it as a passthrough string, avoiding the type resolution issue.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states that `datetime.date`, `datetime.time`, and `datetime.datetime` can only be used with the Pydantic Data Converter. The default `DefaultPayloadConverter` does not natively handle these types, even though it handles many JSON-serializable types. Installing Pydantic and passing `pydantic_data_converter` when creating the Client is the documented solution. This is a common production surprise when developers assume Python's standard datetime types are universally supported.

questions.question: You need IPv4Address values to work correctly inside `Optional[IPv4Address]` and `List[IPv4Address]` type hints in a Python workflow. You implement a custom `EncodingPayloadConverter` for IPv4Address and register it at the front of the converter chain. During testing, deserialization of `Optional[IPv4Address]` fields returns raw strings instead of `IPv4Address` objects. What is the root cause?

choices.text: The custom `EncodingPayloadConverter` is never called for nested or parameterized types; only the outermost type's converter is invoked during deserialization.

Collection and union type hints are resolved by the JSON converter, not by earlier encoding converters. Without customizing the JSON converter via a `JSONTypeConverter`, the inner type hint is not dispatched to your converter.
Pydantic's type system is required for any optional or collection type resolution; the `CompositePayloadConverter` alone cannot handle parameterized generics.
The `from_payload` method on the custom converter raises an assertion error when `type_hint` is `None`, which happens for wrapped types like `Optional`, causing silent fallback to string.

questions.explanation: When a value is part of a collection, union, or optional type, the JSON converter is responsible for resolving the inner type hint—not the earlier encoding converters. A standalone `EncodingPayloadConverter` placed first in the chain handles top-level values of that type, but collection members are serialized as JSON and must be handled at the JSON layer. The fix is to customize the JSON converter using a `JSONTypeConverter` (and optionally a `JSONEncoder`), as shown in the documentation's IPv4Address example. This is a subtle but critical distinction between the two customization approaches.

questions.question: A team adds a new custom Payload Converter to their Go Temporal workers and passes it as the sole argument to `NewCompositeDataConverter(YourCustomPayloadConverter())`. After deploying, workflows that previously accepted standard structs (serialized as JSON) begin failing during replay because existing history events can no longer be deserialized. What is the most direct explanation?

choices.text: Removing `NewNilPayloadConverter()` from the chain causes `nil` values already stored in history to be passed to `YourCustomPayloadConverter()`, which panics and breaks replay.
Replacing the entire default converter chain means `NewJSONPayloadConverter()` is no longer present, so history payloads encoded with JSON encoding cannot be deserialized by the new chain.
Temporal validates converter chains on worker startup and rejects configurations missing the default converters, raising a configuration error before replay begins.
The custom converter attempts to deserialize the binary proto metadata header present in all history events, causing a type assertion failure during replay.

questions.explanation: History events are immutable and stored with the encoding chosen at the time they were written. If existing events were serialized using the JSON encoding (via `NewJSONPayloadConverter()`), removing that converter from the chain means the workers can no longer deserialize those payloads during replay, breaking determinism. The documentation explicitly warns about this: when adding a custom converter, you should keep the defaults (especially `NewJSONPayloadConverter()` as a catch-all) and insert your custom converter just before the JSON fallthrough. Replacing the entire chain with a single custom converter is a common but dangerous migration mistake.

questions.question: A Python workflow is defined with three separate input parameters of different types. Over time, the team needs to add new optional fields. After adding a new parameter at the end of the function signature with a default value, older workflow executions that were serialized before the change start failing during replay. What practice, recommended in the Temporal documentation, would have prevented this?

choices.text: Using `*args` and `**kwargs` in workflow definitions to avoid positional binding, allowing the serializer to skip unrecognized parameters without error.
Defining workflows, signals, queries, and updates with a single `dataclass` or Pydantic model parameter, so new optional fields can be added without breaking serialization compatibility.
Bumping the workflow type name with a version suffix when adding parameters, so old executions continue using the prior type's serialization schema.
Keeping all parameters as `bytes` and performing custom deserialization inside the workflow, so the payload converter never encounters unfamiliar types during replay.

questions.explanation: The Python documentation explicitly recommends using a single `dataclass` or Pydantic model parameter for workflows, signals, queries, and updates. When all inputs are bundled into a single model, new optional fields with defaults can be added without changing the function signature, preserving backward compatibility with existing serialized history. Multiple positional parameters create brittleness: adding, removing, or reordering them changes the serialized payload structure and breaks replay of in-flight executions. This is one of the most impactful data modeling practices for long-running workflow compatibility.

questions.question: Your team uses a Payload Codec for encryption alongside a custom `CompositePayloadConverter` in Go. A security review flags that some payloads visible in the Temporal Web UI are not encrypted, even though the Payload Codec is configured on the worker. Which architectural detail of Temporal's converter pipeline best explains this behavior?

choices.text: Payload Codecs are applied after Payload Converters during serialization, so if the Payload Converter emits a payload with an unrecognized encoding, the Codec skips it to avoid corrupting the data.

Payload Converters and Payload Codecs are independent layers. If the Codec is not configured on the Remote Data Converter used by the Temporal Web UI, raw (unencoded) payloads are displayed directly from history.
The `NewNilPayloadConverter()` always emits payloads with a null encoding marker that bypasses the Codec layer, so nil-valued fields are never encrypted regardless of codec configuration.
Payload Codecs only operate on activity input and output, not on workflow input, search attributes, or memo fields, which are stored in plaintext by design.

questions.explanation: Temporal's converter architecture separates Payload Converters (type serialization) from Payload Codecs (encoding transformations like compression or encryption). The Codec is applied at the SDK boundary on the client/worker side. The Temporal Web UI can only decrypt payloads if a Remote Data Converter (a codec server) is configured and accessible to the UI. Without it, the UI reads raw (potentially encrypted) bytes from history and cannot display decoded content—or if encryption isn't being applied at all, payloads are stored in cleartext. The documentation explicitly notes that Payload Converters can be customized independently of a Payload Codec, emphasizing they are separate concerns in the pipeline.

questions.question: A Go service team wants to add support for a new custom type `MoneyAmount` alongside existing Protobuf and JSON-serialized workflow data. They place `YourCustomPayloadConverter()` after `NewProtoPayloadConverter()` but before `NewJSONPayloadConverter()` in the chain. During load testing, they observe that MoneyAmount fields inside structs serialized as JSON are not correctly decoded. What is the most precise explanation?

choices.text: The custom converter is registered after the Protobuf converter, so Protobuf messages containing embedded MoneyAmount fields are intercepted before the custom converter can process the nested value.

The `CompositeDataConverter` only calls each converter once per top-level payload, not recursively for nested fields. A `MoneyAmount` nested inside a JSON-encoded struct is decoded entirely by `NewJSONPayloadConverter()`, which has no knowledge of the custom type.
Placing the custom converter before `NewJSONPayloadConverter()` causes a conflict: both converters claim the same `application/json` encoding header, making deserialization non-deterministic.
The Go SDK requires that custom converters implement `CanConvert()` to return `true` for sub-fields; without this interface, nested type conversions are silently skipped.

questions.explanation: In Temporal's Composite Data Converter, each converter operates on a complete top-level Payload, not on fields within a payload. When a Go struct containing a `MoneyAmount` field is serialized, the entire struct is handed to the first converter that claims it—in this case `NewJSONPayloadConverter()`. The JSON converter serializes the whole struct including the `MoneyAmount` field, but it only knows how to decode types registered with the standard Go JSON encoder. The custom `PayloadConverter` can only handle `MoneyAmount` when it is the top-level value passed to the chain. For nested custom types, the solution is to implement custom JSON marshaling on the containing struct (e.g., `MarshalJSON`/`UnmarshalJSON`), not to add a separate Payload Converter entry in the chain.

questions.question: A Python Temporal worker handles workflow inputs that include `IPv4Address` values both as standalone parameters and embedded inside `List[IPv4Address]` and `Optional[IPv4Address]` fields. An engineer implements a custom `EncodingPayloadConverter` that handles `IPv4Address` directly and inserts it before the default converters. After deploying, standalone `IPv4Address` arguments serialize correctly, but `List[IPv4Address]` fields raise deserialization errors during workflow replay. What is the root cause?

choices.text: The `EncodingPayloadConverter` approach only intercepts top-level payload boundaries; when `IPv4Address` is nested inside a collection, the JSON converter handles the entire list as a single payload and its default encoder/decoder has no knowledge of `IPv4Address`, so round-tripping fails. The fix is to customize the JSON converter's encoder and `JSONTypeConverter` instead.

Python's `CompositePayloadConverter` applies converters in parallel for collection types rather than sequentially, so inserting a custom converter first doesn't affect how list elements are dispatched. You must register the converter with a separate `CollectionPayloadConverter` registry to handle nested types.

Replay fails because the workflow history stores the encoding metadata as `text/ipv4-address`, but during replay the worker loads the default `DataConverter` first before applying custom converters, causing a metadata mismatch that surfaces only for parameterized generic types.

The issue is that `Optional[IPv4Address]` uses `Union` internally, and the `CompositePayloadConverter` skips custom converters for `Union` type hints due to a known SDK limitation. Using `dataclasses.replace` to patch the converter class name is insufficient; you must subclass `DataConverter` directly to handle unions.

questions.explanation: When a `List[IPv4Address]` or `Optional[IPv4Address]` is serialized, the `CompositePayloadConverter` doesn't decompose the collection into individual elements—it treats the entire value as a single unit and dispatches it to whichever `EncodingPayloadConverter` claims it. Because `IPv4Address` isn't JSON-serializable by default, the fallback JSON converter wins for the collection, serializing it as a JSON array of stringified addresses. On deserialization, the JSON converter receives the raw JSON string but has no `JSONTypeConverter` that can map the string back to `IPv4Address` instances, so it returns raw strings instead, causing type errors during replay. The fix documented in Temporal's Python SDK is to customize the JSON converter itself—replacing the `JSONPlainPayloadConverter` with one that has a custom `json.JSONEncoder` (to serialize `IPv4Address` as a string) and a custom `JSONTypeConverter` (to reconstruct `IPv4Address` from that string based on the type hint). This is a critical production insight: the `EncodingPayloadConverter` approach is sufficient for top-level values of a specific type, but for types that appear inside collections, optionals, or unions, you must extend the JSON layer because that's where nested type dispatch happens. The two strategies are not interchangeable—they operate at different levels of the converter pipeline.

questions.question: A Go team uses a `CompositeDataConverter` built with converters in this order: `NilPayloadConverter`, `ByteSlicePayloadConverter`, `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter`, `ProtoPayloadConverter`, `JSONPayloadConverter`. They add a new activity that returns a custom struct `OrderResult` which implements `proto.Message` (via a generated protobuf). During a canary deploy, old workers replay histories written by new workers and panic with a deserialization error on `OrderResult` payloads. New workers, however, replay old histories fine. What is the most likely cause?

choices.text: New workers serialize `OrderResult` with `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter` (encoding: `json/protobuf`), producing JSON with proto field name conventions. Old workers, using the same converter order, attempt `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter` first but fail to resolve the concrete proto type from the payload metadata because the type URL references a package path not yet registered in the old binary's proto registry.

The `ByteSlicePayloadConverter` intercepts the proto message before `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter` can claim it, because `proto.Message` implements the `[]byte` interface through its `Marshal` method. Old workers therefore receive raw binary payloads and cannot deserialize them using the proto converters.

Old workers fail because Temporal's history replay validator rejects payloads whose encoding metadata doesn't match a converter registered at worker startup, and the old binary was compiled without the `OrderResult` proto file, causing a schema registry mismatch at the cluster level before the worker even attempts deserialization.

New workers serialize `OrderResult` with `ProtoPayloadConverter` (binary proto, encoding: `binary/protobuf`) because it appears after `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter` in the chain and takes precedence for types that satisfy `proto.Message`. Old workers, missing the proto descriptor in their registry, cannot unmarshal the binary payload and panic.

questions.explanation: The `CompositeDataConverter` applies converters in order, stopping at the first that returns a non-nil payload. For a `proto.Message`, `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter` appears before `ProtoPayloadConverter`, so it wins and encodes the value as JSON with protobuf field naming conventions, writing `json/protobuf` as the encoding metadata along with the full proto type URL. When an old worker replays this history, it reads the encoding metadata, correctly routes to `ProtoJSONPayloadConverter`, but then tries to unmarshal the payload into the correct concrete proto type. The Go proto runtime uses a global type registry keyed by the fully-qualified proto type name. If the old binary was compiled without the `OrderResult` proto descriptor—or with a different proto package path—the type URL cannot be resolved, causing a panic. New workers replaying old histories succeed because old histories didn't contain `OrderResult` payloads. This scenario illustrates why converter ordering AND backward-compatible proto registration matter together in rolling deployments: the encoding choice is deterministic based on converter order, but successful deserialization depends on both the encoding metadata and the proto type registry being consistent across worker versions. Teams should treat proto type registration as part of their API contract during canary rollouts, not just the wire format.

questions.question: A Python workflow accepts a Pydantic v2 model `ShipmentRequest` as its sole input parameter and is deployed with `pydantic_data_converter`. Six months later, the team adds a new optional field `priority: Optional[str] = None` to `ShipmentRequest`. Existing workflow executions that are mid-flight (started before the deploy) continue running. When those old executions receive a signal that re-reads the original workflow input from a side-effect, they successfully deserialize it. However, when the team adds a second new field `scheduled_date: datetime.date` (no default value) and redeploys, old mid-flight executions begin failing on the same signal handler with a `ValidationError`. Which of the following correctly explains the interaction between Pydantic data conversion, workflow history replay, and schema evolution?

choices.text: The Pydantic data converter stores the full Pydantic schema version hash alongside each payload. When a new required field is added, the schema hash changes, and the converter raises `ValidationError` rather than attempting deserialization with the stale hash, regardless of whether a default is provided.

Adding `priority: Optional[str] = None` succeeds because Pydantic's validator tolerates missing optional fields with defaults during deserialization. Adding `scheduled_date: datetime.date` without a default makes the field required in Pydantic's model, so when the converter attempts to reconstruct `ShipmentRequest` from the JSON payload stored in history (which lacks `scheduled_date`), Pydantic raises `ValidationError` because a required field is absent.

Both fields cause `ValidationError` during replay because Temporal's replay engine re-executes the workflow from the beginning and re-deserializes all inputs on every replay, including inputs from history events that predate the schema change. The `pydantic_data_converter` enforces strict mode by default, so any schema difference—even optional fields—triggers a validation error.

The failure occurs because `datetime.date` is only supported by the Pydantic data converter when wrapped in a Pydantic model field annotated with `Field(...)`. Using it as a bare type hint causes the converter to fall through to the default JSON converter, which cannot handle `datetime.date` and raises `ValidationError` instead of a serialization error.

questions.explanation: The Pydantic data converter serializes workflow inputs to JSON at the moment the workflow starts and stores that JSON in the workflow history. When a signal handler (or any code path) later deserializes the original input—whether from a query, a side effect, or an activity result—the converter reads the stored JSON and attempts to reconstruct the Pydantic model. Pydantic v2's validation requires all fields without defaults to be present in the input data. `priority: Optional[str] = None` has a default, so deserialization of old JSON (which lacks the field) succeeds—Pydantic fills in `None`. `scheduled_date: datetime.date` has no default, making it a required field; when Pydantic tries to validate the stored JSON (which predates the field's addition and therefore lacks it), it raises `ValidationError`. This is a fundamental schema evolution challenge that exists independently of Temporal: the serialized form in history is immutable, but the deserialization schema changes with your code. The Temporal documentation's advice to use a single `dataclass` or Pydantic model with fields that have defaults is precisely to avoid this: all new fields on long-running workflows must have defaults to maintain backward compatibility with payloads already stored in history. Additionally, `datetime.date` requires the Pydantic data converter (as the docs explicitly note), reinforcing that date/time types and required-field discipline are both critical when evolving schemas for long-running workflows.

questions.question: A Go team has a production `CompositeDataConverter` that places a custom `YourCustomPayloadConverter` just before `NewJSONPayloadConverter()` as their JSON fallthrough. They later realize some workflow inputs are large protobuf messages and add a Payload Codec for compression on top. A new engineer on the team wants to add encryption as well and proposes creating a *second* `CompositeDataConverter` that wraps the first one and adds an encryption step. What is the fundamental architectural flaw in this proposal, and what is the correct approach?

choices.text: A `CompositeDataConverter` cannot be nested inside another `CompositeDataConverter` because the Go SDK enforces a singleton Data Converter per client via a global registry. Wrapping one inside another causes a registration conflict at client initialization. The correct approach is to flatten all converters into a single `NewCompositeDataConverter` call ordered from most-specific to JSON fallthrough.

The proposal conflates the Payload Converter layer (type-specific serialization to Payload) with the Payload Codec layer (Payload-to-Payload transformations like compression and encryption). Payload Converters determine *how* a Go value becomes a Payload; Payload Codecs operate on already-serialized Payloads. Encryption should be implemented as a second `PayloadCodec` chained with the existing compression codec, not as a wrapping Data Converter.

The proposal is valid architecturally, but fails at runtime because the inner `CompositeDataConverter` serializes the value to a Payload, and then the outer `CompositeDataConverter` attempts to serialize that Payload again, producing double-encoded data. The fix is to ensure the outer converter's first-in-order converter recognizes `Payload` structs and passes them through without re-encoding.

The flaw is that `CompositeDataConverter` applies converters sequentially until one succeeds, so a wrapping converter would always route to `NilPayloadConverter` on the inner converter's output (a non-nil `Payload` struct), bypassing encryption. Adding encryption requires forking the SDK and modifying the `CompositeDataConverter.FromPayload` pipeline directly.

questions.explanation: Temporal's Data Conversion architecture has two distinct, independent layers: Payload Converters and Payload Codecs. Payload Converters (assembled into a `CompositeDataConverter`) are responsible for type-specific serialization: they take a Go value and produce a `Payload` (a binary blob with encoding metadata). Payload Codecs are Payload-to-Payload transformations applied *after* conversion; they receive an already-serialized `Payload` and return a transformed `Payload`—this is the correct layer for compression, encryption, or any other cross-cutting concern that should apply regardless of the value's type. The documentation makes this separation explicit: 'Payload Converters can be customized independently of a Payload Codec.' The engineer's proposal to nest `CompositeDataConverter` instances treats encryption as a type-serialization concern, which it isn't—encryption doesn't depend on the Go type being serialized, it applies uniformly to all payloads. The correct architecture is a `DataConverter` configured with: (1) the existing `CompositeDataConverter` for type-specific serialization, and (2) a `CodecDataConverter` wrapping two `PayloadCodec` implementations—one for compression and one for encryption—applied in the desired order. This keeps the layers separate, ensures encryption applies to all payloads regardless of type, and doesn't interfere with the converter selection logic. Conflating these two layers in production leads to subtle bugs where some value types bypass encryption or where codec-transformed payloads are re-encoded by an outer converter.
